title: Traffic Stop Drug and Firearm Arrest Records

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Ohio Open Records Law, I hereby request the following records:

Pursuant to the Ohio Public Records Act, I request all releasable public records related to traffic stops from January 1, 2025 to present that resulted in the seizure of illegal narcotics, firearms, or both. This request includes:

Body-worn camera footage
Dash camera footage
CAD/dispatch logs
Arrest reports
Incident reports
Evidence photographs
911 calls, if applicable

I request that all records be provided electronically if available.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 business days.

Sincerely,

SAD Footage

title: Contract Request: Interfolio

communications.subject: North Carolina Public Records Law Request: Contract Request: Interfolio
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the North Carolina Public Records Law, I hereby request the following records:

The most recent contract, governing, base, or master license agreement with Elsevier relating to the licensing of Interfolio;
All related terms and conditions, terms of service, amendments, addenda, appendices, attachments, product terms, MOUs, MOAs, data processing agreements, and/or letters of intent; 
All financial documents or records of financial transactions, exchanges or obligations related to the contractual relations with Elsevier for faculty information system technology;
and
Assessment documentation including, but not limited to, the Higher Education Community Vendor Assessment Toolkit (HECVAT). 

Responsive materials include all contracts, agreements, amendments, appendices, invoices, and any other documents listed above that incorporate language governing terms of use of Interfolio.

The scope of this request should encompass more than just direct contracts or renewals between the university and Elsevier; please incorporate all applicable agreements and supporting records facilitated through consortium entities (such as Lyrasis).

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 business days.

Sincerely,

mo

The annual report required under Ohio Code Section 122.17(D)(6) for Anduril's Arsenal-1.

Additionally, I also request any information reasonably necessary for the applicable TIRC [Tax Incentive Review Council] to make the determinations required under Section 5709.85 of the Ohio Revised Code, to evaluate the compliance by the Owner and Tenant for Anduril's Arsenal-1 project with the Community Reinvestment Agreement, including any tax return documentation if requested by the applicable TIRC and information necessary to perform its review with the nondiscriminatory hiring policies developed by the County under Section 5709.832 of the Ohio Revised Code if requested by the applicable TIRC. 

Any responsive records may redact individually identifying confidential information such as social security numbers and employee names.

communications.subject: California Public Records Act Request: 2020-2025 Fremont CA Red Light Camera Reports VEH 21455.5(i)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the California Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

Documents received by the Judicial Council of California that describe the operation of the automated traffic enforcement system as described in VEH 21455.5(i) for the jurisdiction of Fremont CA (Fremont Police Department) between 2023 and 2025

Vehicle Code 21455.5(i) states:

"(i) A manufacturer or supplier that operates an automated traffic enforcement system pursuant to this section shall, in cooperation with the governmental agency, submit an annual report to the Judicial Council that includes all of the following information if this information is in the possession of, or readily available to, the manufacturer or supplier:

"(1) The number of alleged violations captured by the systems they operate.

"(2) The number of citations issued by a law enforcement agency based on information collected from the automated traffic enforcement system.

"(3) For citations identified in paragraph (2), the number of violations that involved traveling straight through the intersection, turning right, and turning left.

"(4) The number and percentage of citations that are dismissed by the court.

"(5) The number of traffic collisions at each intersection that occurred before and after the installation of the automated traffic enforcement system."

REQUESTS: VEH 21455.5(i) reports received by the Judicial Council of California for the jurisdiction of Fremont CA (Fremont Police Department) for the years of 2023, 2024, and 2025.

For reference, a sample record that would be responsive for the year of 2014 is attached to this request

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

communications.subject: Thank you for your request. (Autoreply)
communications.communication:
Thank you for contacting the Judicial Council of California’s Public Access to Judicial Administrative Records (PAJAR) team. This message is an automatic reply to let you know that we received your request.

Please read the message below because it has information that may help you.

*** PLEASE DO NOT REPLY to this e-mail message. Responses to this automatic reply are not monitored.***

WHAT TO EXPECT


  *   If you are asking for court case records—for example, a court order, divorce records, name change records, criminal history records— this message will be the only response you receive. Please read the section below, The PAJAR Team Cannot Provide These Records, to find information that may be helpful.


  *   If you are asking for judicial administrative records from the Judicial Council, the Supreme Court of California, or a California Court of Appeal, the PAJAR team will send you a response within about 10 days.


  *   Here are some examples of judicial administrative records: signed contracts, final audit reports, budget and spending reports, reports to the Legislature, and procurement records. You can find more information about judicial administrative records at courts.ca.gov/cms/rules/index/ten/rule10_500<https://courts.ca.gov/cms/rules/index/ten/rule10_500> and at courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records<https://courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records>.


  *   The PAJAR team may ask you for more information to be sure it understands your request. The PAJAR team may also notify you if it needs more time to complete a search.


  *   When work on your request is complete, the PAJAR team will notify you whether there are disclosable responsive records or not. If there are disclosable responsive copies, the PAJAR team will provide electronic copies, if possible.

THE PAJAR TEAM CANNOT PROVIDE THESE RECORDS

>   Court Case Records—Superior Courts of California

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of superior court case records. If you want records filed in a specific superior court case, you must contact the court directly. California has 58 superior courts, one in each county. You can find links to each court’s website at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Each superior court clerk is responsible for that court’s records.

Some superior court websites allow the public to search and view some types of case records online. If a court’s website does not allow you to search and view case records online, you must call or visit the court clerk’s office for help. Superior courts post their telephone numbers and street addresses on their websites. Some also post e-mail addresses.

Examples of records that you must request by contacting a superior court:

·         Divorce decrees;

·         Traffic citations;

·         Name change records;

·         Wills;

·         Criminal case records (for example, orders imposing sentences and judgments); and

·         Restraining orders.

You will find more information about some of these topics in the message below.

>   Court Case Records—Supreme Court of California and California Courts of Appeal

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of appellate court case records. You can find information about specific cases filed with the Supreme Court of California or with the California Courts of Appeal at appellatecases.courtinfo.ca.gov<http://appellatecases.courtinfo.ca.gov/>.

You can review opinions that the Supreme Court of California and the California Courts of Appeal issued in specific cases at courts.ca.gov/opinions.htm<http://www.courts.ca.gov/opinions.htm>.

You can find the following Supreme Court of California case records online:

·         Legal briefs filed in cases argued before the court: supreme.courts.ca.gov/case-information/briefs-argued-cases<https://supreme.courts.ca.gov/case-information/briefs-argued-cases>; and

·         Official minutes going back three years: supreme.courts.ca.gov/case-information/minutes<https://supreme.courts.ca.gov/case-information/minutes>.

To view or request copies of records filed in a specific case before the Supreme Court of California, you must call or visit the court. Supreme Court contact information is posted at supreme.courts.ca.gov/about-court/court-locations-and-contacts<https://supreme.courts.ca.gov/about-court/court-locations-and-contacts>.

To view or request copies of records filed in specific cases before a California Court of Appeal you must call or visit the court. You can find a list of the six Courts of Appeal at appellate.courts.ca.gov<https://appellate.courts.ca.gov/>. On the left side of that page, select the court where the case is filed. Then scroll down to select “Contact Us.” On that page you will find court telephone numbers and location addresses.

If you have a Court of Appeal case number, but you do not know the court where the case is filed, please visit appellate.courts.ca.gov<https://appellate.courts.ca.gov/>. That page lists the letters that begin the case numbers for each of the Courts of Appeal.

Information about California appellate court cases also is available at your local law library, which may provide free public access to computer-assisted legal research systems such as LexisNexis or Westlaw. To learn more about local public law libraries, and to locate one near you, visit selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/court-basics/law-libraries<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/court-basics/law-libraries>.

>   Court Case Records—United States Federal Courts


The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of records filed in cases before federal courts located in California. To find information about those federal courts—including court telephone numbers and location addresses for those courts—please visit:

  *   www.cand.uscourts.gov<http://www.cand.uscourts.gov> (district court, Northern California);
  *   www.casd.uscourts.gov<http://www.casd.uscourts.gov> (district court, Southern California);
  *   www.caed.uscourts.gov<http://www.caed.uscourts.gov> (district court, Eastern California);
  *   www.cacd.uscourts.gov<http://www.cacd.uscourts.gov> (district court, Central California); and
  *   www.ca9.uscourts.gov<http://www.ca9.uscourts.gov> (Courts of Appeal).

>   Birth or Death Certificates

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of birth or death certificates. The registrar or recorder in the county where the birth or death occurred would have these records.

You can find a list of all California county registrars and recorders, and their contact information, at www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/County-Registrars-and-Recorders.aspx<http://www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/County-Registrars-and-Recorders.aspx>.

For information about how to request a birth or death certificate, please visit the website of California’s Department of Public Health, at www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/Vital-Records.aspx<http://www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CHSI/Pages/Vital-Records.aspx>.

>   Domestic Violence Information

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of restraining or protective orders. You can find Information about where and how to ask for help in domestic violence circumstances on the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/DV-restraining-order<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/DV-restraining-order>.

If you want copies of a restraining or protective order, you must contact the superior court where the case is filed. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

>   Criminal Background Records

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of criminal history records. You must request copies from the superior court where the criminal case is filed. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

Federal, state, and local law enforcement agencies are responsible for investigating criminal activity. If you suspect criminal activity is taking place, please contact law enforcement authorities. You can find a list of California Law Enforcement Agencies at post.ca.gov/le-agencies<https://post.ca.gov/le-agencies>.

>   Name Change Records

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of name change records. The California State Archives stores some name change records. Please visit the State Archives website, at www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact<http://www.sos.ca.gov/archives/about-archives/contact/> to learn more. You can also call the California State Archives reference desk at 916-653-2246.

Information about the process for completing a name change can be found on the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/name-change<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/name-change>.

>   Traffic Citations

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of traffic citations. Each superior court handles traffic matters independently and is responsible for its own records. If you have questions about a traffic ticket, please contact the superior court listed on the ticket (i.e., the “Notice to Appear”). You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

You can find general information about traffic tickets on the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/traffic<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/traffic>.

>   Wills

The PAJAR team cannot provide copies of a will for someone who has died. If a copy of the will has been filed or lodged with a superior court, you must contact the superior court to request a copy. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

For general information about wills, estates, and probate matters, please visit the California Courts Self-Help website at selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/wills-estates-probate<https://selfhelp.courts.ca.gov/wills-estates-probate>.

>   Jury Duty Information

The PAJAR team cannot answer questions about jury duty. If you have questions about a juror summons, or want to be excused from jury duty, please contact the superior court that issued the jury summons. You can find links to all 58 superior court websites at courts.ca.gov/find-my-court<https://courts.ca.gov/find-my-court>. This page also contains links to the pages on each court’s website that has information about juror service. Court websites list court telephone numbers and locations.

You can find general information about jury service in California by visiting courts.ca.gov/courts/jury-service<https://courts.ca.gov/courts/jury-service>.

We hope this information is helpful. Thank you for your interest in the California judicial branch.

Sincerely,
Public Access to Judicial Administrative Records
Legal Services | Leadership Services Division
Judicial Council of California
455 Golden Gate Avenue
San Francisco, California 94102-3688
415-865-7796 | PAJAR@jud.ca.gov<mailto:PAJAR@jud.ca.gov> | courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records<https://courts.ca.gov/policy-administration/public-records>

title: KING COUNTY DIRECTORS' ASSOCIATION – Board of Directors Meeting(s) Records (2025)

communications.subject: Washington Public Records Act Request: KING COUNTY DIRECTORS' ASSOCIATION – Board of Directors Meeting(s) Records (2025)
communications.communication:
KING COUNTY DIRECTORS' ASSOCIATION – Board of Directors Meeting(s) Records (2025)

June 08, 2026

via email to: Kristen Deskin, Human Resources Manager and Public Records Officer <kdeskin@kcda.org>

To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Washington Public Records Act [RCW 42.56], I hereby request the following records:

1. Produce copies of all "Records and Writings" of all minutes, complete meeting packets, resolutions, audio or video recordings, executive session logs, and presentations, inclusive, for every meeting of the KCDA Board of Directors for the year 2025, including all records provided to the Board by Executive Directors Bart Powelson or David Mahalko regarding litigation updates, vendor awards, or funding-out contract terminations.

DEFINITION OF RECORDS AND WRITINGS

"RECORDS AND WRITINGS" as defined by RCW 42.56.010(4): any handwriting, typewriting, printing, photostating, photographing, and every other means of recording any form of communication or representation including, but not limited to, letters, words, pictures, sounds, or symbols, or combination thereof, and all papers, maps, magnetic or paper tapes, photographic films and prints, motion picture, film and video recordings, magnetic or punched cards, discs, drums, diskettes, sound recordings, and other documents including existing data compilations from which information may be obtained or translated.

PRODUCTION PROCESSING AND DELIVERY OF ELECTRONIC RECORDS

Please provide all records in their native electronic format. Please do not instead create new records by printing electronic files then scanning and printing the newly scanned prints.

To preserve metadata and ease review, please produce as follows:

Emails: PST (Microsoft Outlook) or MBOX containers.

Chats/Texts/IM: Native exports where supported; otherwise, CSV/JSON with fields for sender, recipients, timestamps (UTC), message/thread IDs, and attachments (export attachments in original formats).

Calendars: ICS where possible; or PDF plus any attachments.

Documents/attachments: Original/native formats (DOCX, XLSX, PPTX, PDF, etc.).

Logs/Reports: CSV/JSON preferred.

Delivery: Direct electronic transmission via email attachments or open (no login/registration/password) download link hosted by your agency (e.g., SharePoint/OneDrive public link). Please do not use third-party portals that require creating an account or logging in. A single or series of .zip files is acceptable.

If any records cannot be produced in the above formats, please produce them in their native format with associated metadata intact.

METADATA REQUESTED

Where reasonably available, please include customary metadata fields such as: From, To, CC, BCC, Subject, Sent/Received Date-Time (with timezone/UTC), Message-ID, Conversation/Thread-ID, file names, file sizes, hash values (if held), and folder/location paths.

SEARCH DETAILS AND RECORDS PRESERVATION

Please identify the systems searched e.g. (Exchange/Outlook/Office 365, Teams, mobile devices, MDM archives, JustFOIA/CivicPlus admin, helpdesk, file shares) and the custodians.

Please describe the search methods/terms used.

If any potentially responsive sources were not searched or are no longer available, please explain why and what preservation steps were taken.

USE OF THIRD-PARTY PORTALS NOT MANDATORY

Please note that under Washington’s Public Records Act, use of a third-party “portal” is not mandatory for requestors. Agencies may not condition access to records on the creation of an account, the use of a login, or the acceptance of third-party vendor terms.

Third-party portals (such as GovQA, NextRequest, JustFOIA, or similar systems) often:

Require account creation or login credentials;

Restrict downloads, viewing times, or re-access;

Remove or disable links after a limited period;

Place records behind commercial platforms operated by for-profit corporations.

These restrictions unlawfully burden and condition access to public records. The Public Records Act forbids agencies from imposing access barriers beyond those authorized by statute.

RCW 42.56.080(2): An agency “shall not deny a request for identifiable public records solely on the basis that the request is overbroad.” Agencies must provide the “fullest assistance” and “most timely possible action” to requestors.

RCW 42.56.550(1): Courts are directed to liberally construe the PRA to ensure access and strictly construe exemptions against withholding.

Fisher Broadcasting-Seattle TV LLC v. City of Seattle, 180 Wn.2d 515, 528 (2014): Agencies must produce records in a “reasonably usable format when feasible.”

Mechling v. City of Monroe, 152 Wn. App. 830, 841 (2009): PRA is to be liberally construed in favor of disclosure and cannot be restricted by agency practices.

Because of this, I respectfully decline use of any third-party portal. Please provide all responsive records via:

Direct electronic email attachment to this requestor’s address, or a direct, open (no password, no registration required) cloud-based download link hosted by your agency.

Do not register this requestor with, nor direct this requestor to, any third-party commercial portal, including but not limited to FOIAOnline, GovQA (a Granicus Corporation), NextRequest, FOIAExpress, JustFOIA, FOIADirect, or any similar online service.

Do not transmit any portion of this request, or responsive records, to any for-profit vendor or portal. Doing so would unlawfully condition access to public records and contravene both RCW 42.56.080 and RCW 42.56.550.

PUBLIC RECORDS REQUESTS SUBMITTED VIA EMAIL

This public records request is being submitted via email, consistent with Washington’s Public Records Act.

RCW 42.56.080(2) provides in relevant part: “Agencies shall honor requests received in person during an agency’s normal office hours, or by mail or email, for identifiable public records unless exempted by provisions of this chapter. No official format is required for making a records request; however, agencies may recommend that requestors submit requests using an agency-provided form or web page.”

Accordingly:

A request submitted by email is a valid, enforceable method of making a PRA request.

Agencies may recommend, but cannot require, the use of an online portal or official form.

Any refusal to honor this request as submitted would directly conflict with RCW 42.56.080 and the agency’s duty under RCW 42.56.100 to provide the “fullest assistance to requestors” and adopt reasonable rules that do not “unreasonably hinder or delay” access.

Therefore, this request is to be honored and fulfilled by your agency via email, as submitted. Should the agency fail to process this request in compliance with statute, relief will be sought pursuant to RCW 42.56.550 in the form of judicial review, penalties, and attorney’s fees.

INSTALLMENTS AND FEE ESTIMATES

Please provide an initial acknowledgment within five business days (RCW 42.56.520) with a reasonable time estimate for each installment, and

A cost estimate if fees are anticipated (please itemize and explain).

NISSEN AFFIDAVIT AND ALL DEVICE SEARCH REQUIRED

Pursuant to Nissen v. Pierce County, 183 Wn.2d 863 (2015), and its progeny, this request specifically includes all responsive public records contained on personal devices or personal accounts (including personal cell phones, text/SMS/MMS, personal email accounts, cloud storage, instant messaging apps, and social media accounts) of any agency employee, official, or contractor where public business may have been conducted.

To comply with this requirement, please:

Conduct a comprehensive all-device search, including both agency-issued and personally owned devices/accounts used for public business.

Require each potentially responsive custodian to execute a Nissen affidavit or sworn certification affirming:

1. They conducted a diligent search of all personal and agency devices/accounts reasonably likely to contain responsive records;

2. The methods used to conduct the search (e.g., search terms, accounts checked, device types); and

3. Whether any responsive records were located, preserved, and produced.

Produce all responsive records located in such searches along with the executed affidavits.

Failure to obtain and provide sworn Nissen affidavits or equivalent certifications from custodians may constitute an incomplete search in violation of RCW 42.56.520 (requirement for a reasonable and adequate search) and may expose the agency to relief under RCW 42.56.550.

EXEMPTION LOGS 

Please ensure that all redactions or exemptions claimed by your agency in the production of responsive records are accompanied by a complete and detailed exemption log.

Exemptions logs at minimum must contain the following:

1. Identify the valid legal reason (statutory citation) for each exemption;

2. Specify the location of each redaction within the record produced (via footnote, coded reference, or margin notation);

3. State the number of pages or records withheld in their entirety, if any; and

4. Demonstrate that only the minimal exempt portion of any record has been withheld, as required by law.

The Washington Public Records Act requires agencies to justify each withholding or redaction:

RCW 42.56.210(3): “Agency responses refusing, in whole or in part, inspection of any public record shall include a statement of the specific exemption authorizing the withholding of the record (or part) and a brief explanation of how the exemption applies to the record withheld.”

RCW 42.56.210(1): Exemptions must be narrowly construed.

Washington courts have repeatedly enforced this duty:

Rental Housing Ass’n of Puget Sound v. City of Des Moines, 165 Wn.2d 525, 538 (2009): Agencies must provide specific explanations for each exemption; conclusory or vague justifications are insufficient.

Sanders v. State, 169 Wn.2d 827, 845 (2010): “The burden of proof is on the agency to establish that refusal to permit public inspection and copying is in accordance with a statute.”

Resident Action Council v. Seattle Housing Auth., 177 Wn.2d 417, 433 (2013): Agencies must disclose the non-exempt portions of records and cannot withhold more than necessary.

Therefore, I request that your agency provide an itemized exemption log with each installment of records produced, in compliance with RCW 42.56.210(3) and the above case law, ensuring transparency and accountability in the redaction process.

COMMERCIAL PURPOSE DECLARATION

This public records request is not being made for any commercial purpose. The Washington State Public Records Act (PRA) prohibits agencies from disclosing “lists of individuals” for commercial purposes.

RCW 42.56.070(8) states: “This chapter shall not be construed as giving authority to any agency to give, sell, or provide access to lists of individuals requested for commercial purposes….”

Accordingly:

This request does not seek a “list of individuals.”

This request is not made for any commercial purpose.

This declaration satisfies all requirements of RCW 42.56.070(8) regarding prohibitions on lists and commercial purposes.

Warning: The PRA does not authorize agencies to require requestors to complete additional “commercial purpose declarations” when the request does not seek a list of individuals. Such requirements improperly condition access to public records in violation of RCW 42.56.080(2) (requests must be honored by mail or email, no official format required) and RCW 42.56.100 (agencies must provide the fullest assistance to requestors and may not adopt rules that unreasonably hinder or delay access).

Washington courts have consistently rejected agency attempts to impose conditions not found in statute:

Mechling v. City of Monroe, 152 Wn. App. 830, 841 (2009): The PRA is a strongly worded mandate for broad disclosure; agencies cannot erect unauthorized barriers.

SEIU Local 925 v. University of Washington, 193 Wn.2d 860, 875 (2019): The PRA is to be liberally construed to promote access, and exemptions must be narrowly applied.

Therefore, no additional commercial purpose declaration will be completed for this request. Any attempt to impose such a requirement would be an unlawful restriction of rights under RCW 42.56.

PRESERVATION OF PUBLIC RECORDS *** ANTICIPATE LITIGATION *** WAC 44-14-03005 RETENTION OF PUBLIC RECORDS

WAC 44-14-03005 (Retention of Public Records) makes clear that public records are subject to retention schedules, and that unlawful destruction can constitute a crime.

RCW 40.16.010 and RCW 40.16.020 criminalize the unlawful mutilation, destruction, or concealment of public records.

RCW 42.56.100 expressly prohibits an agency from destroying any public record after a public records request has been made for that record, even if the record would otherwise be scheduled for lawful destruction.

In addition, agencies have heightened retention obligations when litigation is reasonably anticipated. Records potentially relevant to actual or anticipated litigation must be preserved until both the public records request and the litigation have been fully resolved.

Legal Consequences of Premature Destruction: In Yacobellis v. City of Bellingham, 55 Wn. App. 706, 780 P.2d 272 (1989), the Court of Appeals held that an agency violates the Public Records Act and is subject to attorneys’ fees and statutory penalties when it destroys responsive records after receiving a public records request.

By contrast, it is not a PRA violation if a record was destroyed prior to the agency’s receipt of a request, provided the destruction was lawful under retention schedules. See Bldg. Indus. Ass’n of Wash. v. McCarthy, 152 Wn. App. 720, 218 P.3d 196 (2009); West v. Dep’t of Natural Resources, 163 Wn. App. 238, 258 P.3d 78 (2011).

Washington courts have emphasized that the Public Records Act (RCW 42.56) and the state’s records retention laws (RCW 40.14) are separate but complementary. Agencies must comply with both.

Accordingly:

Your agency must preserve all potentially responsive records immediately upon receipt of this request.

No responsive records may be altered, deleted, or destroyed until this request has been fully resolved and any related litigation constraints have been lifted.

Any unlawful destruction of responsive records may expose the agency and its officials to criminal liability (RCW 40.16), civil penalties, and mandatory attorneys’ fees (RCW 42.56.550(4)).

RECORDS INSTALLMENTS - ORDER OF PRODUCTION

If production of responsive records will be completed in multiple installments, production in reverse chronological order is strongly preferred to the extent supported by native system exports, provided it does not delay the delivery of installments.

DIGITAL COMPRESSION AND FILE MINIMIZATION REQUESTED IN ACCORDANCE WITH RCW 42.56

Pursuant to RCW 42.56.080(2), please provide all responsive electronic records in the fewest number of individual files, preserving native formats wherever possible. Where appropriate, please use free digital compression (.zip or equivalent) to consolidate responsive records into the smallest number of installments for efficiency and economy.

This approach reduces agency burden, promotes prompt disclosure, and minimizes potential costs to the requestor, consistent with the Public Records Act’s directive that agencies provide records in the most “reasonably locatable form” and “fullest assistance to requestors.” See RCW 42.56.100 (agency duty to adopt reasonable rules for providing fullest assistance); RCW 42.56.120(2)(b) (fees must reflect actual costs, not used to discourage access).

Washington courts have consistently held that agencies must facilitate access in a practical, non-burdensome manner. See Fisher Broadcasting-Seattle TV LLC v. City of Seattle, 180 Wn.2d 515, 528 (2014) (agencies must provide records in a usable format when reasonably feasible); Mechling v. City of Monroe, 152 Wn. App. 830, 841 (2009) (PRA is to be liberally construed in favor of disclosure). Producing consolidated, digitally compressed files satisfies these obligations by reducing unnecessary fragmentation and excess per-page charges.

Accordingly, I request that each installment be produced as a single compressed .zip file (or as few as technically necessary), containing all responsive records in native format, to ensure both compliance with RCW 42.56 and an economical calculation of any applicable public records fees.

COMMUNICATION VIA EMAIL

Please send all requests for clarification, status updates, and production of responsive electronic documents via direct email reply.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. We look forward to receiving your response to this request within 5 business days, as the statute requires.

Good Luck.

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the New York Freedom of Information Law, I hereby request the following records:

Pursuant to the New York Freedom of Information Law, Public Officers Law §§ 84–90, I request copies of the following records concerning Request for Proposals #2026-009, EMS Billing Software / Services, issued by the Albany County Sheriff's Office / Albany County Division of Purchasing, which I understand was awarded to Zoll Billing:

Complete copies of all proposals, bids, and submissions received from every vendor in response to RFP #2026-009, including all attachments, exhibits, pricing schedules, and supplementary materials.
All evaluation and scoring criteria, rubrics, weighting methodologies, or rating instruments used to assess the proposals, including any documents establishing the criteria prior to evaluation.

All internal and external correspondence — including emails, memoranda, letters, and instant or text messages — among or between committee members, County staff, the Sheriff's Office, the Division of Purchasing, and any vendor, relating to the evaluation, scoring, ranking, or award of RFP #2026-009.
Any notice of award, award recommendation or justification, and the final executed contract or agreement with the selected vendor (Zoll Billing) arising from this RFP.
A list or roster identifying the members of the evaluation/selection committee for this RFP and their titles or roles.

I request that records be provided in electronic format (searchable PDF or native file format) and delivered electronically where possible.
If any portion of any responsive record is withheld or redacted, please (a) identify each record withheld in whole or in part, (b) cite the specific FOIL exemption under Public Officers Law § 87(2) relied upon for each withholding, and (c) release all reasonably segregable, non-exempt portions. Where a third party has asserted a trade-secret claim under § 87(2)(d), please indicate that such a claim was made and the date of any notice given.
If you anticipate that fees for responsive records will exceed $25, please contact me with an estimate before processing the request. Please respond within the five business days required by Public Officers Law § 89(3).

Sincerely,

xxlawdogxx

 - 2.A) If your agency shares data with AVCC/PSDEX, please provide query logs of who has accessed your agency's data and what reason/case number they provided while searching your agency's records. Please provide in CSV or XLSX format where available. If you are unable to see logs of how your agency's data is used by other agencies within AVCC, please indicate so.

 - 3.B) Configuration Explainer Documents: Typically documents with details on what data LexisNexis recommends that you contribute, and what they recommend you exclude. --
